title: Cryptographic Protection — BitLocker or Disk Encryption Status Drops

grandmaExplanation: We track endpoint encryption compliance for SC-13 cryptographic protection — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742), Intune HEC.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-BitLocker/Operational` or inventory JSON.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Normalize status strings; (2) exclude expected lab hosts via lookup; (3) auto-quarantine workflow optional; (4) map to SC-28 overlap for at-rest.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=os sourcetype=win:bitlocker_status earliest=-24h protection_status="Off"
| stats count by host, volume, reason
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Cryptographic Protection — BitLocker or Disk Encryption Status Drops (SC-13)** — Tracks endpoint encryption compliance for SC-13 cryptographic protection.

Documented **Data sources**: `WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-BitLocker/Operational` or inventory JSON. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742), Intune HEC. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: os; **sourcetype**: win:bitlocker_status. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=os, sourcetype=win:bitlocker_status, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host, volume, reason** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes by Processes.dest | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Cryptographic Protection — BitLocker or Disk Encryption Status Drops (SC-13)** — Tracks endpoint encryption compliance for SC-13 cryptographic protection.

Documented **Data sources**: `WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-BitLocker/Operational` or inventory JSON. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742), Intune HEC. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Endpoint.Processes` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (unencrypted volumes), Single value (non-compliant hosts)
